metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add per-content-element grep anchors to AC7's proving command so each of the five required ADR sections is independently verified (e.g., `grep -qi 'prompt.injection' …`, `grep -qi 'gray.zone\|grey.zone\|example' …`, `grep -qi 'external tool' …`, `grep -qi 'bounded\|honest guarantee' …`), or explicitly state that content completeness is verified by the reviewer at PR time and note that the existing command is a structural-only check.
findings.finding: AC7's proving command (`test -f docs/adr/0043-... && grep -q 'operator-attested' CLAUDE.md`) verifies only file existence and a single token in CLAUDE.md, but AC7 specifies five distinct required content elements for the ADR: (1) two-kinds model + where DONE evidence lives, (2) bounded meaning of signed verdict, (3) haste-not-deception threat model WITH an explicit note on indirect-prompt-injection prior art and why the command/attestation split is acceptable, (4) concrete-vs-vague discriminator with ≥3 canonical gray-zone examples, (5) why external tools/collector are out of scope. A stub ADR with one occurrence of 'operator-attested' passes the proof command, so the five content requirements are unverifiable at close time.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the AC7 proof block with a compound command that anchors each required section:
```
test -f docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md \
  && grep -qi 'operator-attested' docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md \
  && grep -qi 'prompt.inject' docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md \
  && grep -qi 'gray.zone\|grey.zone\|example' docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md \
  && grep -qi 'external tool\|out of scope' docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md \
  && grep -qi 'bounded\|honest guarantee\|ticket.*codebase' docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md \
  && grep -q 'operator-attested' CLAUDE.md
```
Alternatively, add a note: 'structural proof only — content completeness verified by PR reviewer against the five AC7 items.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the AC5 proof to include a human-readable inline test or a gold-set entry that explicitly demonstrates the case-conditional rubric works for both operator-attested and codebase-verifiable cases (e.g. add gold_set entries covering the operator-attested/vague and operator-attested/concrete grounding expectations).
findings.finding: The plan's four new eval dataset cases for `completion-verifier.eval.yaml` add `[operator-attested]`-tagged AC items. The existing `test_new_eval_specs.py` runs `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` against the `completion-verifier` spec — this test will re-run and cover the new cases automatically. However, `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` enforces that the dataset is 'balanced' between `FIRE_EXPECTS` and `NOFIRE_EXPECTS` labels. The current spec has 3 `fail` + 3 `pass` cases. Adding 4 new cases (a: pass, b: fail, c: pass, d: fail = 2 pass + 2 fail) keeps balance. BUT the plan also calls for updating the `finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded` rubric (AC5) to be case-conditional. This rubric change is a textual change to the eval YAML, not a new scorer — `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` does NOT validate rubric text. The plan's proof for AC5 is only `grep -qi 'operator-attested' …eval.yaml`, which does not confirm the case-conditional logic actually works correctly for the new cases.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC3's proof (or AC1's greps) to include a grep verifying that `completion_verifier.md` contains the instruction to populate `remediation` for unmet operator-attested criteria (e.g. `grep -qi 'remediation' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md`).
findings.finding: AC3's text requires three things — the `remediation` field in `VerdictFinding`, the schema documentation, AND the prompt instructing the verifier to populate it for an unmet operator-attested criterion — but the Proof only covers the first two (unit test + grep contracts.py + make test). No grep or check verifies that `completion_verifier.md` actually contains the remediation-population instruction, leaving that sub-requirement unverified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3's Proof line: `&& grep -qi 'remediation' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md` so the prompt requirement is mechanically verified alongside the code requirement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC7's proof to verify at least the three most distinctive required elements, e.g. add greps for 'injection' (prior-art discussion), 'gray' or 'example' (gray-zone examples), and 'external' or 'out of scope' (collector rationale) in the ADR file.
findings.finding: AC7's text demands rich ADR content — indirect-prompt-injection prior art, ≥3 canonical gray-zone examples, why external tools/collector are out of scope, the bounded-verdict meaning, and the haste-not-deception threat model — but the Proof only checks file existence (`test -f docs/adr/0043-…`) and a single CLAUDE.md grep. The substantial content requirements in the AC body go entirely unverified.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC7 Proof with: `test -f docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md && grep -qi 'injection' docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md && grep -qi 'gray' docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md && grep -qi 'external' docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md && grep -q 'operator-attested' CLAUDE.md`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the AC6 proof command from `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier` to `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier` (the registered binary is `rebar`, not `rebar-eval`).
findings.finding: The AC6 proof instruction names `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier` as the CLI command to run the live eval, but no `rebar-eval` binary exists — `pyproject.toml` registers only `rebar` and `rebar-mcp`; the correct command is `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier`.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier` with `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier` in AC6; the `--live` flag (if required for a live run) should also be confirmed against `rebar prompt eval --help`.

findings.finding: AC5's verification method is 'Verified by reading the eval spec; covered by the AC5 validate_eval_spec command' — but the specific claim (that the grounding rubric accepts ticket-text citation for operator-attested findings) is a semantic/behavioral property that validate_eval_spec does not test, leaving AC5 with no machine-executable proving command.
findings.location: AC5 (grounding rubric relaxation) — 'Verified by reading the eval spec'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC3: Explicitly specify that `remediation` is added to the 'Each finding:' bullet list in `completion_verifier.md`'s '## Verdict and findings' section, with the exact instruction text the verifier uses to populate it for an unmet operator-attested criterion (e.g., 'remediation: the accepted next move — record the reference id/URL, the observed outcome, and when, as a comment on the ticket').
findings.finding: The plan instructs the verifier prompt to populate `remediation` on `VerdictFinding` for an unmet operator-attested criterion (AC3: 'the prompt instructs the verifier to populate it'), but the existing `completion_verifier.md` has no `remediation` field in its output schema description, and the new prompt additions are only described in the plan's prose — the structural link between the prompt's output schema section and the new `remediation` field is never specified (e.g., whether `remediation` is added to the 'Each finding:' bullet list in the prompt).
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `## Verdict and findings` section under 'Each finding:': '- `remediation` (operator-attested unmet only): the accepted next move — record the reference (change URL/id), the observed outcome (votes/logs/console value), and when, as a comment on the ticket; absent for codebase-verifiable findings and for MET criteria.'

findings.finding: The AC4c eval case (CV-attestation-with-injection) requires 'a scorer/rubric asserting the PASS was reached by crediting the factual specific while ignoring the command,' but the plan never specifies which scorer name, whether it is a new deterministic scorer or an LLM-judge, or how it is wired. No matching scorer name exists in eval_scorers.py REGISTRY. The `expect: pass` + `no_false_fail_on_complete` scorer pair would fire only on PASS/FAIL verdict — it cannot distinguish *why* a PASS was reached (factual credit vs. command obeying).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC5: Clarify HOW the rubric becomes case-conditional given the harness's single-string `rubric:` field — specify that the implementer should write a single compound rubric text that names both the operator-attested carve-out (source/ticket citation accepted) and the codebase-verifiable requirement (file/symbol required), rather than implying a per-case branching mechanism that the harness does not support.
findings.finding: AC5 specifies that the `finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded` llm-judge rubric should be 'case-conditional' (operator-attested cases accept `source`/ticket-text citations; codebase-verifiable cases require file/symbol citations), but the eval harness has no per-case rubric mechanism — `validate_scorer` accepts a single `rubric:` string applied uniformly to every case. The plan does not specify how case-conditionality is implemented, so an implementer has no actionable path: a static compound rubric string (covering both branches in prose) is the only available mechanism, but the plan says 'case-conditional' without stating that.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'is made **case-conditional**' with: 'is updated to a single compound rubric string that explicitly names both branches — for an `[operator-attested]` case, a `source`/ticket-text citation counts as valid grounding; for a codebase-verifiable case, a file/symbol citation is required. Both conditions live in one rubric string (the harness applies a single rubric uniformly); the case-type context is provided by the case fixture itself so the grader can apply the correct branch.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC5: Specify the structural mechanism by which the llm-judge grader receives per-case kind information (operator-attested vs. codebase-verifiable) to apply the conditional rubric — e.g., whether the rubric is a template that interpolates a case-level `kind` field, whether the eval YAML gains a per-case `rubric_override`, or whether the grader prompt is constructed to include the case's `ticket_context` prefix as the classification signal.
findings.finding: AC5 specifies a 'case-conditional' llm-judge rubric for `finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded` that applies different grounding bars depending on whether a case is operator-attested or codebase-verifiable, but the eval harness supports only a single flat `rubric: string` per scorer with no per-case rubric override or metadata injection mechanism. The plan never specifies the structural mechanism by which the grader receives the per-case kind classification, leaving the executor unable to implement the conditionality.
findings.suggested_fix: The lowest-friction path (no harness changes): make the rubric self-contained by instructing the grader to classify the case from the finding's own citation kinds — 'if the only citation is kind=source, treat ticket-text as valid grounding; if a kind=file citation is present or expected and absent, require it'. This removes the need for external case metadata and works with the existing flat-rubric harness. Document this convention explicitly in AC5.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in AC3 how `remediation` will be documented in `completion_verdict.schema.json`'s `findings` items without modifying `common.schema.json` — e.g. add a description sibling to the `$ref` on the items object, or annotate the `findings` array description — so the implementer doesn't need to infer the schema authoring approach.
findings.finding: The plan asserts it will 'document the per-finding `remediation` in the `findings` items description' in `completion_verdict.schema.json` while keeping `common.schema.json` untouched. The current `findings.items` in `completion_verdict.schema.json` is a bare `{"$ref": "common.schema.json#/$defs/finding"}` (line 17-18) — a pure `$ref` that leaves no room for inline sibling keywords under JSON Schema 2020-12 (a `$ref` alongside other keywords in 2020-12 IS legal, unlike draft-07, but an implementer unfamiliar with the draft version may add `remediation` to `common.schema.json` instead to avoid uncertainty). The plan doesn't specify the schema authoring approach (allOf, sibling description, or array-level description annotation).
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `findings` array's `description` string in `completion_verdict.schema.json`: 'Each FAIL finding may carry a `remediation: string` (operator-attested guidance) as an additional property (allowed by `additionalProperties: true` on the shared finding def).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an assertion to the AC3 unit test (or a dedicated eval-case rubric entry) that verifies `remediation` is non-null/non-empty in the verifier's output when an operator-attested criterion is NOT MET — e.g., assert the serialized verdict for CV-operator-attested-vague contains a non-empty `remediation` on the failing finding.
findings.finding: AC3 specifies a unit test that proves `VerdictFinding` carries `remediation` and it 'round-trips through the model + serialized verdict,' but no test or eval scorer verifies that the verifier ACTUALLY POPULATES `remediation` in its output for an unmet operator-attested criterion. The existing scorers (`recall_on_incomplete`, `no_false_fail_on_complete`, `emits_valid_verdict`) check verdict pass/fail and schema validity — since `remediation` is optional, a null value passes schema validation. Per-finding remediation is a new user-facing behavior (the reason for AC3/What item 4), yet the only failure path covered is 'criterion not met' at the verdict level, not 'remediation field absent when it should be present.'
findings.location: AC3 (unit test scope) + AC4 eval case (b) CV-operator-attested-vague + What item 4
findings.suggested_fix: In the unit test fixture for AC3, include a sample `VerdictFinding` with `remediation` populated and assert its value survives the round-trip. Then add a rubric line to the `finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded` judge (or a new `remediation_populated_on_fail` rubric) in the eval spec that checks: for case CV-operator-attested-vague, the failing finding's `remediation` field names the accepted next move (reference ID / observed outcome / timestamp). Alternatively, in AC6's operator-attested live run, explicitly record whether `remediation` was non-null for the failing finding.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the AC4 fixture design and updated prompt explicitly require the verifier to emit a `kind: source` citation (citing the ticket attestation text) for FAIL findings on operator-attested cases, so `emits_valid_verdict`'s citation-presence check is satisfied alongside `recall_on_incomplete`.
findings.finding: The `emits_valid_verdict` scorer (eval_scorers.py:155-171) requires every FAIL finding to carry ≥1 citation, and it applies to ALL completion-verifier eval cases including the new operator-attested ones. The plan's AC4 FAIL cases (`CV-operator-attested-vague`, `CV-unmarked-fallback-codebase`) will be gated by this scorer simultaneously with `recall_on_incomplete`. If the LLM returns a correct FAIL verdict but omits source citations from the finding (plausible for operator-attested scenarios where grounding is in ticket text), the case fails `emits_valid_verdict` — but this is indistinguishable from an incorrect verdict in the eval harness. The plan states behavioral proof 'rides on the expect labels through the EXISTING deterministic scorers' without acknowledging this compound gating constraint or specifying that the prompt/fixture must produce source-cited findings for operator-attested FAIL cases.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to AC4 specifying that for operator-attested FAIL cases the verifier must cite the ticket attestation as `kind: source` (e.g. `{kind: source, description: 'attestation in ticket description: …'}`), and verify the updated `completion_verifier.md` prompt instructs this behavior for unmet operator-attested criteria.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC3/What-4: Consider adding a deterministic post-hoc fill in `reconcile_verdict` (or a new `reconcile_operator_attested` step) that sets `finding['remediation']` to the accepted next-move text for any finding whose criterion is tagged `[operator-attested]` and is missing a remediation, so the guidance is guaranteed rather than LLM-dependent.
findings.finding: The plan adds `remediation: str | None` to `VerdictFinding` and says 'the prompt instructs the verifier to populate it for an unmet operator-attested criterion,' but this relies entirely on the LLM emitting the optional field — there is no deterministic guardrail that fills it in post-hoc. The existing top-level `remediation` has a deterministic guardrail (`reconcile_verdict`); the per-finding one does not. A structured-output model frequently omits optional fields, especially on a newly-added field the LLM has no training signal for.

The eval solver (`eval_solver.py`) has no mechanism to seed ticket COMMENTS into disposable eval fixtures — it only writes `ticket_context` as the ticket description. The three new operator-attested eval cases (CV-operator-attested-concrete, CV-operator-attested-vague, CV-attestation-with-injection) require attestation evidence that the verifier reads via `show_ticket`; the plan describes this as 'a ticket_context comment naming a change id + vote outcome + timestamp,' but the solver calls `rebar.create_ticket(description=ticket_context)` and never calls `rebar.comment()` on the disposable ticket. If the implementer embeds the attestation inside the description (the only available vehicle), the test works but validates description-embedded attestations — not the comment-as-evidence path that production operators will use. If the implementer tries to seed a real comment, the solver needs extension that is out of scope and unspecified.
findings.location: AC4 (four new eval dataset cases) / `src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_solver.py` lines 193–201 / `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/completion-verifier.eval.yaml`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `comments: list[str]` field to eval case data and extend `eval_solver.py`'s `completion-verifier` arm: after `create_ticket`, iterate `case.get('comments') or []` and call `rebar.comment(tid, body, repo_root=root)` for each. This faithfully seeds the production evidence channel and makes the fixture self-documenting. If description-embedding is intentional instead, note it explicitly in the eval spec comment and rename the AC4 description from 'a ticket_context comment' to 'ticket_context text that includes an attestation'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the prior synonyms being retired and add a sweep of existing docs (at minimum `infra/runbooks/two-vote-gate-rollback.md`) to the Scope, updating or cross-referencing them to use the canonical terms `codebase-verifiable` and `operator-attested`.
findings.finding: The plan declares 'all prior synonyms retired' for the two criterion kinds but does not enumerate the prior synonyms or audit which existing files carry them. The runbook `infra/runbooks/two-vote-gate-rollback.md` is explicitly cited in the Why section as evidence of existing completed work, yet it is absent from the Scope update list — leaving it a confirmed stale-reference candidate after the terminology change.
findings.location: Canonical terminology section; Scope (IN list); Why section referencing `infra/runbooks/two-vote-gate-rollback.md`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope IN list: `infra/runbooks/two-vote-gate-rollback.md` — update any occurrence of prior synonyms to the canonical terms (or add a one-line note mapping old terms to new). Optionally add a `grep -r` step in the implementation checklist to catch any other files carrying the retired synonyms.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the next available ADR number at implementation time (currently two 0041s exist; 0042 may already be taken); update the ADR filename and all references in AC7, Scope, and `completion_verifier.md` link accordingly.
findings.finding: ADR `docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md` does not exist — the ADR directory only goes up to 0041. The plan names this file in both the Scope section and AC7, and AC7's proof command `test -f docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md` would fail against any pre-existing file.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the ADR number from a hardcoded `0043` to a placeholder (e.g. `004X`) with a note 'assign the next available ADR number at implementation time,' or audit the current ADR sequence and confirm 0042/0043 are free.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `test_runner_structured_schema.py` (or whichever test pins `completion_verdict` model ↔ schema) requires every Pydantic field to appear in the schema shape, and if so explicitly add a `remediation` description to the `findings[].items` object in `completion_verdict.schema.json` (e.g. as a sibling `description` on the `$ref` item, or via an inline `allOf`).
findings.finding: The plan adds `remediation: str | None` to `VerdictFinding` in `contracts.py`, but `test_runner_structured_schema.py` (or equivalent schema-pin test) pins the `completion_verdict` Pydantic model against `completion_verdict.schema.json`. Adding a new field to `VerdictFinding` without also adding it to the `findings[].items` shape in the schema will cause the schema-pin test to fail, since the pin test verifies model ↔ schema fidelity. The plan says `common.schema.json` is NOT modified and the `findings` items use `$ref: common.schema.json#/$defs/finding` with `additionalProperties: true` — but the pin test likely checks that every Pydantic field is represented.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before finalizing the command/attestation split rule, document (in the plan or the ADR draft) the specific prior art reviewed (e.g., indirect prompt injection mitigation research) and confirm the 'checkable fact vs verdict control' heuristic is considered adequate given that literature — rather than relegating this review to post-design ADR documentation.
findings.finding: The command/attestation injection-guard split is a security-adjacent architectural decision committed in the plan body, yet the plan treats the relevant indirect-prompt-injection prior art as something the ADR (0043) will document after the fact — not as input that shaped the design before commitment. If that prior art review had been done first, it might have revealed that the proposed 'reports a checkable fact' vs 'controls the verdict' distinction is harder for an LLM to enforce reliably than the plan assumes (a well-known finding in injection-mitigation research).
findings.location: Why / Rationale sections; AC7 (ADR requirement to document 'indirect-prompt-injection prior art and why haste-not-deception makes the command/attestation split acceptable')

findings.finding: AC4 case (b) requires the eval spec's scorer/assertion to verify that the resulting finding's `remediation` field is 'non-empty and carries the record-proof coaching', but the stated proving command (`validate_eval_spec(strict=True) == []`) only checks spec structural validity — it cannot verify that the scorer actually asserts on the remediation field content (fixture invalidity, defect type 4).

AC4's `CV-operator-attested-vague` case requires the assertion 'finding's `remediation` field is non-empty and carries the record-proof coaching', but no deterministic scorer in the `REGISTRY` checks per-finding `remediation` content, and the plan does not name a new scorer to add. The `test_new_eval_specs.py` auto-coverage check (`validate_eval_spec(strict=True)`) only validates scorer names are registered — it cannot enforce this per-finding field assertion unless a new scorer is registered.

AC4 specifies that the `CV-operator-attested-vague` case must assert 'the resulting finding's `remediation` field [is] non-empty and carries the record-proof coaching,' but the eval framework's gating mechanism works only through registered deterministic scorer names (`known_scorer_names()` in `eval_scorers.py`) or non-gating LLM judges. No registered scorer checks per-finding `remediation`. If the implementer adds a new deterministic scorer name for this assertion without registering it in `REGISTRY`, `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` rejects the spec. If expressed as part of the LLM judge rubric, the assertion is advisory-only (non-gating). The plan specifies neither a new scorer name nor which of the two mechanisms carries this assertion.

The AC4b eval case (CV-operator-attested-vague) requires an assertion that the resulting finding's `remediation` field is non-empty, but no scorer — deterministic or LLM-judge — is defined or named in the plan to enforce this assertion. The REGISTRY in eval_scorers.py has no entry that inspects a finding-level `remediation` field, and the existing `emits_valid_verdict` scorer only checks `citations` presence (line 168-170), not `remediation`. An executor building the eval case has no specified mechanism to satisfy the assertion.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a deterministic scorer `operator_attested_finding_has_remediation` to eval_scorers.py that, for cases tagged with `criterion_kind: operator-attested` (add a case-level metadata key), inspects each FAIL finding for a non-empty `remediation` field. Register it in REGISTRY and name it in the `CV-operator-attested-vague` case's scorer list.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
